verses.text: ‘Elihu also proceeded, and said.’ Elihu’s longest speech is about to begin (chapters 36-37). He will speak of God’s power in nature — lightning, thunder, snow — preparing the way, intentionally or not, for God’s own voice from the whirlwind. The transition from Elihu to the Lord is one of the most artful in Scripture.

‘Suffer me a little, and I will shew thee that I have yet to speak on God’s behalf.’ Elihu claims to be a defender of God. The Hebrew zechut Eloah — ‘God’s right cause’ — names what he aims to advocate. Whether his claim is justified is debated; what is clear is that he believes himself to be doing holy work in arguing for God’s honour.

‘I will fetch my knowledge from afar.’ Elihu intends to range widely — across creation, history, observation — to ascribe righteousness to his Maker. The Hebrew tsedek — righteousness — names what he wants to credit to God. There is something refreshing about Elihu’s vision: God is righteous in ways that go beyond Job’s case.

O princípio bíblico de que Deus nunca tira os olhos dos inocentes ( **2Cr 16.9** ; **Sl 33.18** ; **34.15** ; **1Pe 3.12** ) respondeu à frustração de Jó sobre a vigilância divina ( **Jó 7.17-21** ).\n\n

Se eles ouvirem e obedecerem a Deus, serão abençoados: Este é um princípio bíblico válido de arrependimento (veja **11.13-19** ; **22.21-25** ; **Dt 4.30** ; **Jr 7.23** ; **26.13** ).\n\n

O hebraico yadin pode significar " ele governa" ou "ele nutre" , o que se assemelha a comida em abundância ( **38.26** ; **Sl 65.9-13** ; **104.13-15** , **27-28** ). O significado mais comum para yadin é “julgar,” o que significaria que Deus faz todas as coisas para abençoar seu povo e julgar seus inimigos ( **Jó 37.11-13** ; **38.22-23** ; veja **Gn 6.17** ; **19:24** ; **Êx 9.23-25** ; **Js 10.11-14** ; **1Sm 12.18** ).\n\n
